case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: Model-matrix benchmark for upstream/council combinations on the DES isoxazole protocol.

chemistry_plan.reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
chemistry_plan.bond_formed: C–O and C–C bonds in isoxazole ring
chemistry_plan.bond_broken: N–O bond rearrangement in nitrile oxide generation; loss of H2O from ethyl nitroacetate

feed_streams.reasoning: All reagents are miscible in the DES and must be present together from the start so the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide is immediately trapped by phenylacetylene, minimizing dimerization to furoxan. No incompatibility issues between the two substrates at room temperature.

reagents.name: phenylacetylene
reagents.notes: Terminal alkyne acting as 2π component in [3+2] cycloaddition

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates nitrile oxide in situ via thermal dehydration; 2 equiv used to drive reaction to completion

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.notes: Forms deep eutectic solvent with ethylene glycol; acts as reaction medium promoting nitrile oxide formation

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.notes: Hydrogen bond donor in DES; high boiling point supports 120°C operation

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal tautomerization/dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (the nitrile oxide 1,3-dipole) with loss of water. The DES medium facilitates this process.

Regioselective [3+2] cycloaddition of the nitrile oxide (C≡N⁺–O⁻) with phenylacetylene. The oxygen of the nitrile oxide bonds to the terminal carbon of the alkyne, and the carbon of the nitrile oxide bonds to the internal carbon, giving the 3,5-disubstituted isoxazole regiochemistry.

stream_logic.reasoning: Single premixed feed: both substrates dissolved in DES. Phenylacetylene must be present when nitrile oxide forms to trap it before dimerization. No catalyst or base needed. DES viscosity (~50–100 cP at RT) will decrease significantly at 120°C (~5–10 cP), but preheating the feed or using a syringe pump with back-pressure regulator is advisable. Concentration 0.5 M in phenylacetylene, 1.0 M in ethyl nitroacetate.

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Only one feed stream is needed. Both substrates must be present together before heating because the nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and will dimerize to a furoxan if the dipolarophile (phenylacetylene) is absent. Premixing at room temperature is safe as no reaction occurs below ~80°C.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species; the reaction proceeds under air in the batch protocol.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: No inline quench needed. The batch protocol quenches with water for workup, but in flow this would be done offline. The product ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate is thermally stable.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: well-established nitrile oxide generation from nitroacetate and [3+2] cycloaddition. The DES viscosity at room temperature is a practical concern for flow — may need heated feed lines or dilution. The 15 min residence time at 120°C is very flow-compatible. Using a back-pressure regulator (BPR, ~5–10 bar) is recommended to keep the DES/EG from boiling and to maintain consistent flow. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate compensates for competitive nitrile oxide dimerization. Uncertain about exact DES viscosity under flow conditions — rheological data should be verified experimentally.

final_design_candidate.human_explanation:
## Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream coil reactor:**
- Reactor volume: 297.572 mL
- Tubing: PFA, 1.6 mm ID
- Flow rate: 10.0531 mL/min
- Residence time: 29.6 min
- Temperature: 120.0 °C
- Back-pressure regulator: 5.0 bar

**Feed composition (Stream A):**
- Phenylacetylene: 1.0 equiv (0.067 M)
- Ethyl nitroacetate: 2.0 equiv
- TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent

## Why These Conditions Were Chosen

The design is based on thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition kinetics requiring extended heating for nitrile oxide generation and subsequent [3+2] cycloaddition. The 29.6-minute residence time provides complete conversion (X=1.00) compared to shorter times that yielded only 93-96% conversion. 

The 120°C temperature matches the batch protocol requirement for thermal dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to generate the reactive nitrile oxide intermediate. The single-stream approach prevents premature nitrile oxide dimerization by ensuring the dipolarophile (phenylacetylene) is present when the nitrile oxide forms.

The TBAB/EG deep eutectic solvent system was selected for its thermal stability and ability to promote nitrile oxide formation, similar to ionic liquid-promoted cycloadditions reported in the literature.

## Key Differences from Batch Protocol

1. **Residence time**: 29.6 min vs 4 hours batch (8× intensification)
2. **Concentration**: 0.067 M vs typical 0.5 M batch (diluted for flow dynamics)
3. **Continuous operation**: Eliminates batch heating/cooling cycles
4. **Enhanced heat transfer**: 1.6 mm tubing provides better temperature control than batch vessels
5. **Premixed reagents**: Single feed stream prevents handling of unstable nitrile oxide intermediate

## Engineering Validation Warnings

⚠️ **Flow enhancement factor uncertainty**: The 10× intensification factor is estimated from literature class averages but not experimentally verified for this specific cycloaddition.

⚠️ **Heat transfer validation needed**: Heat transfer coefficient in TBAB/EG at 120°C in 1.6 mm tubing requires experimental confirmation.

⚠️ **BPR critical failure mode**: Risk of TBAB crystallization causing valve blockage during cooling (RPN=54). Install heated BPR housing and 0.5 μm upstream filter.

**Recommended validation experiments:**
1. Run TBAB/EG blank at 120°C for 8 hours monitoring pressure stability
2. Thermal cycling test (25°C → 120°C → 25°C × 10 cycles) checking for precipitation
3. Verify pump seal compatibility with TBAB/EG at 120°C for 48 hours

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.34 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

### Selection Rationale

Candidate 38 wins with a combined score of 0.689, driven by exceptional chemistry performance (0.970) and strong safety margins (0.880). Runner-up 29 scored 0.681 with better kinetics (0.850) but lower safety (0.650), while 23 achieved perfect kinetics (1.000) but poor safety (0.250) due to excessive pressure drop. The winner's 12-minute residence time provides excellent conversion (X=1.00) with manageable pressure drop (7.3 bar vs pump max), while the narrow 0.27mm diameter ensures good heat transfer at 120°C. The balanced objective favored 38's superior chemistry-safety combination over pure kinetics optimization. Key uncertainties include the estimated 10× flow enhancement factor and heat transfer validation at high temperature in the deep eutectic solvent. The first experiment should verify steady-state temperature control and conversion at the design flowrate before optimizing residence time.

### Weighted Score Comparison

| id | combined | chem | kin | fluid | safety | geo | boosted |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **38 ★** | **0.689** | 0.970 | 0.700 | 0.650 | 0.880 | 0.000 | chemistry |
| **29** | **0.681** | 0.950 | 0.850 | 0.720 | 0.650 | 0.000 | chemistry |
| **23** | **0.662** | 0.970 | 1.000 | 0.850 | 0.250 | 0.000 | chemistry |

| **3** | **0.541** | 0.750 | 0.100 | 0.150 | 0.950 | 0.757 | chemistry |

### Resolved Trade-offs

- id=38 vs id=29: id=38 has higher safety score (0.880 vs 0.650) due to lower pressure drop (7.3 vs 48.1 bar) and better BPR margin
- id=38 vs id=23: id=23 achieves perfect kinetics but catastrophic safety (0.250) from 57.3 bar pressure drop in long reactor
- Design envelope comparison: id=38 has moderate flexibility (τ: 8.4-15.6 min, Q: 2.47-4.59 mL/min) vs id=29's narrow window (τ: 1.68-3.12 min)

### Overridden Concerns

- **DR. FLUIDICS**: Re=278 approaching transition regime → *Still well below Re=2300 laminar limit, and narrow diameter ensures good heat transfer*

### Remaining Uncertainties

- Flow enhancement factor of 10× estimated from literature class averages but not experimentally verified for this specific cycloaddition
- Heat transfer coefficient in TBAB/EG deep eutectic solvent at 120°C not validated for 0.27mm diameter

formatted_result.explanation:
## Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream coil reactor:**
- Reactor volume: 297.572 mL
- Tubing: PFA, 1.6 mm ID
- Flow rate: 10.0531 mL/min
- Residence time: 29.6 min
- Temperature: 120.0 °C
- Back-pressure regulator: 5.0 bar

**Feed composition (Stream A):**
- Phenylacetylene: 1.0 equiv (0.067 M)
- Ethyl nitroacetate: 2.0 equiv
- TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent

## Why These Conditions Were Chosen

The design is based on thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition kinetics requiring extended heating for nitrile oxide generation and subsequent [3+2] cycloaddition. The 29.6-minute residence time provides complete conversion (X=1.00) compared to shorter times that yielded only 93-96% conversion. 

The 120°C temperature matches the batch protocol requirement for thermal dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to generate the reactive nitrile oxide intermediate. The single-stream approach prevents premature nitrile oxide dimerization by ensuring the dipolarophile (phenylacetylene) is present when the nitrile oxide forms.

The TBAB/EG deep eutectic solvent system was selected for its thermal stability and ability to promote nitrile oxide formation, similar to ionic liquid-promoted cycloadditions reported in the literature.

## Key Differences from Batch Protocol

1. **Residence time**: 29.6 min vs 4 hours batch (8× intensification)
2. **Concentration**: 0.067 M vs typical 0.5 M batch (diluted for flow dynamics)
3. **Continuous operation**: Eliminates batch heating/cooling cycles
4. **Enhanced heat transfer**: 1.6 mm tubing provides better temperature control than batch vessels
5. **Premixed reagents**: Single feed stream prevents handling of unstable nitrile oxide intermediate

## Engineering Validation Warnings

⚠️ **Flow enhancement factor uncertainty**: The 10× intensification factor is estimated from literature class averages but not experimentally verified for this specific cycloaddition.

⚠️ **Heat transfer validation needed**: Heat transfer coefficient in TBAB/EG at 120°C in 1.6 mm tubing requires experimental confirmation.

⚠️ **BPR critical failure mode**: Risk of TBAB crystallization causing valve blockage during cooling (RPN=54). Install heated BPR housing and 0.5 μm upstream filter.

**Recommended validation experiments:**
1. Run TBAB/EG blank at 120°C for 8 hours monitoring pressure stability
2. Thermal cycling test (25°C → 120°C → 25°C × 10 cycles) checking for precipitation
3. Verify pump seal compatibility with TBAB/EG at 120°C for 48 hours

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.34 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: Model-matrix benchmark for upstream/council combinations on the DES isoxazole protocol.

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal tautomerization/dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (the nitrile oxide 1,3-dipole) with loss of water. The DES medium facilitates this process.

Regioselective [3+2] cycloaddition of the nitrile oxide (C≡N⁺–O⁻) with phenylacetylene. The oxygen of the nitrile oxide bonds to the terminal carbon of the alkyne, and the carbon of the nitrile oxide bonds to the internal carbon, giving the 3,5-disubstituted isoxazole regiochemistry.

stream_logic.reasoning: Single premixed feed: both substrates dissolved in DES. Phenylacetylene must be present when nitrile oxide forms to trap it before dimerization. No catalyst or base needed. DES viscosity (~50–100 cP at RT) will decrease significantly at 120°C (~5–10 cP), but preheating the feed or using a syringe pump with back-pressure regulator is advisable. Concentration 0.5 M in phenylacetylene, 1.0 M in ethyl nitroacetate.

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Only one feed stream is needed. Both substrates must be present together before heating because the nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and will dimerize to a furoxan if the dipolarophile (phenylacetylene) is absent. Premixing at room temperature is safe as no reaction occurs below ~80°C.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species; the reaction proceeds under air in the batch protocol.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: No inline quench needed. The batch protocol quenches with water for workup, but in flow this would be done offline. The product ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate is thermally stable.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: well-established nitrile oxide generation from nitroacetate and [3+2] cycloaddition. The DES viscosity at room temperature is a practical concern for flow — may need heated feed lines or dilution. The 15 min residence time at 120°C is very flow-compatible. Using a back-pressure regulator (BPR, ~5–10 bar) is recommended to keep the DES/EG from boiling and to maintain consistent flow. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate compensates for competitive nitrile oxide dimerization. Uncertain about exact DES viscosity under flow conditions — rheological data should be verified experimentally.

analogies.summary: This photocycloaddition reaction utilizes triplet sensitization with 2,2'-dimethoxythioxanthone (2,2'-MeOTX) as the photocatalyst at 1 mol% loading under 455 nm COB LED irradiation. The flow process operates in acetonitrile at 200°C with a 5-minute residence time and 6.0 mL/min flow rate using a FlowSyn steel coil reactor (30 mL volume) equipped with a 34 bar back-pressure regulator. Flow chemistry enabled daisy-chaining of the initial photochemical step with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions, dramatically improving yield from 16% in batch (2 hours) to 88% in flow with a 24-fold intensification factor. The high-temperature operation required for the thermal cascade was much safer to implement in the continuous flow reactor compared to batch processing.

steps.name: Kinetics & Residence Time
steps.summary: τ = 0.8 min, range [0.5–2.2] min, via analogy (IF = 20×)

steps.name: Reactor Sizing
steps.summary: V_R = 0.38 mL, d = 1.00 mm, L = 0.48 m, Pe = 9 (⚠ axial dispersion)
warnings: Pe = 9 < 100 — significant axial dispersion. Reactor behaves closer to CSTR than PFR. Reduce d (smaller d → higher Pe) or accept lower conversion efficiency.

steps.name: Mass Transfer
steps.summary: t_mix = 101.3 s, Da = 39.377 — Mass-transfer limited — mixing is the bottleneck

values.interpretation: Mass-transfer limited — mixing is the bottleneck

warnings: Da = 39.4 ≫ 1: severely mass-transfer limited. Need micromixer, smaller d, or pre-mixed streams.
assumptions: D = 1e-09 m²/s (typical for small organic molecules in liquid)

steps.name: Heat Transfer
steps.summary: Q_gen = 0.4430 W, Q_rem = 4.5003 W, Da_th = 0.0984 — Thermally safe

steps.name: Back-Pressure Regulator
steps.summary: BPR REQUIRED: 5.0 bar (Gas-liquid system detected — BPR mandatory to maintain gas solubility (Henry's law: C_gas = H·P) and prevent uncontrolled degassing.)
equations: C_{\mathrm{gas}} = H \cdot P_{\mathrm{partial}}\quad\text{(higher BPR} \Rightarrow \text{more dissolved gas)}

steps.summary: STY = 33.2000 mol/(L·h), P = 12.450 mmol/h, ṅ_lim = 0.0333 mmol/min, startup waste = 1.1 mL, IF = 20×

streams.pump_role: single feed containing all reagents premixed in DES
streams.reasoning: From Chemistry Plan stream_logic: Single premixed feed required because nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and will dimerize if phenylacetylene is not present. Concentration adjusted from batch 0.5 M to 0.067 M based on calculation results showing reactor concentration of 0.0667 M.

proposal.mixing_order_reasoning: From Chemistry Plan: Only one feed stream needed. Both substrates must be present together before heating because nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and will dimerize to furoxan if dipolarophile is absent.
pre_reactor_steps: Preheat feed line to 80°C to reduce DES viscosity before entering reactor
post_reactor_steps: Cool product stream to room temperature
Offline workup with water extraction and dichloromethane as in batch protocol
proposal.chemistry_notes: Thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition requiring 120°C for nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate, followed by [3+2] cycloaddition with phenylacetylene. Key design considerations: (1) DES viscosity requires preheating and BPR to maintain flow, (2) Single stream prevents nitrile oxide dimerization, (3) Short residence time (0.8 min) prevents product decomposition, (4) Calculations show mass-transfer limitation (Da=39.4) but acceptable for this reaction type, (5) BPR mandatory to prevent DES/EG boiling at 120°C.

reasoning_per_field.residence_time_min: Calculation estimated 0.8 min (range 0.5-2.2 min). Analogy 1 used 5 min for photocycloaddition but our thermal reaction is faster. Analogy 2 used 179 min for different chemistry. Using calculated 0.8 min as it matches batch kinetics with 20x intensification factor.
reasoning_per_field.flow_rate_mL_min: Calculation result: 0.5 mL/min total flow rate to achieve 0.8 min residence time in 0.38 mL reactor volume. Verified: τ = V/Q = 0.38/0.5 = 0.76 min ≈ 0.8 min.
reasoning_per_field.temperature_C: Same as batch (120°C) - required for nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate. All analogies used different temperatures for different chemistry types.
reasoning_per_field.concentration_M: Calculation shows reactor concentration of 0.0667 M vs batch 0.5 M. This dilution accounts for flow dynamics and ensures proper heat/mass transfer in narrow tubing.
reasoning_per_field.BPR_bar: 5.0 bar required by calculation due to gas generation (water loss in nitrile oxide formation). Lower than analogies but sufficient for this system.
reasoning_per_field.reactor_type: Coil reactor as specified in Chemistry Plan stage 1. Analogies 1&2 used coil/tubular reactors successfully for thermal reactions.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_material: PFA chosen for high temperature (120°C) compatibility and chemical inertness to DES. Not photochemistry so transparency not required. Analogy 1 used steel but PFA safer for DES.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_ID_mm: 1.0 mm from calculation (d=1.00 mm) giving acceptable pressure drop (0.002 bar) and laminar flow (Re=11). Analogy 3 used 1.2 mm for similar thermal reaction.
reasoning_per_field.reactor_volume_mL: 0.38 mL from calculation (V_R = 0.38 mL) to achieve target residence time. Much smaller than Analogy 1 (30 mL) due to faster kinetics.
reasoning_per_field.light_setup: Empty string - this is purely thermal reaction, no photochemical activation. Chemistry Plan confirms requires_light: false.
reasoning_per_field.wavelength_nm: null - no light required for thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition
